title: Cat Chewed Marijuana Plant: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat chewed a marijuana plant, stay calm. Learn about THC toxicity symptoms, the urgency level, and how to help your feline friend recover.
content_html:
What is Marijuana Toxicity?

When a cat chews on or ingests parts of a marijuana plant (Cannabis sativa), they are exposed to THC (tetrahydrocannabinol), the psychoactive compound that causes intoxication. Because cats have more cannabinoid receptors in their brains than humans, they are significantly more sensitive to the effects of the plant, which can lead to neurological and physical distress.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for marijuana plant ingestion is generally Medium. While ingestion is rarely fatal, the symptoms can be quite severe and uncomfortable for your cat. You should contact your veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately to discuss the amount consumed and any symptoms your cat is exhibiting. Medical intervention may be necessary if your cat is extremely lethargic, unable to walk, or showing signs of respiratory distress.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo of the plant and the damaged area can be incredibly helpful. Since different parts of the plant (such as the buds versus the leaves) contain varying concentrations of THC, seeing exactly what was consumed allows the medical team to better estimate the potential severity of the toxicity and choose the most effective treatment plan.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of marijuana ingestion in cats?
faqs.answer: Symptoms often include lack of coordination (stumbling), dilated pupils, dribbling urine, slowed heart rate, and hypersensitivity to sound or touch.

faqs.question: How long will it take for my cat to recover?
faqs.answer: Most cats recover within 12 to 24 hours, but recovery time depends on the amount ingested and the individual cat's metabolism.

faqs.question: Can I treat my cat at home?
faqs.answer: You should never attempt home treatment without professional advice. While some mild cases just need a quiet, dark room, a vet must confirm that your cat's vital signs are stable.
